text: The patient was a thirty-two-year-old female at the twentieth week of pregnancy. She presented with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting and constipation.
She had a previous abortion and she had no previous surgery.
Abdominal ultrasonography revealed dilation of ileum without any inflammation around appendix.
Abdominal MRI was performed for further evaluation and revealed diffuse dilation of jejunum and ileum consistent with ileus.
She had six days of bowel rest and conservative medical treatment without any response.
Later on, laparoscopic exploration was planned.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table following the induction of general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation.
The surgeon inserts the first trocar into the left upper quadrant using Hassan's technique, followed by insufflation of the peritoneal space.
Under direct laparoscopic vision, the surgeon inserts a second 5mm trocar into the abdominal cavity, facilitating further exploration and surgical intervention.

Laparoscopic exploration shows an enlarged uterus occupying the pelvis and lower abdomen. Elongated loose bands are visible attached to the distal edge of the omentum above the uterus, which are divided. Additional third and fourth five-millimeter trocars are inserted to enhance surgical maneuverability.
The surgeon begins laparoscopic exploration to locate the Treitz's ligament, navigating the abdominal cavity with the aid of previously inserted trocars.
Despite efforts to locate the trite's ligament, the enlarged uterus obstructs its visibility during laparoscopic exploration.
The surgeon observes extremely fragile and edematous bowel loops, likely due to preobstructive dilation, within the laparoscopic field.

The surgeon uses a bowel grasper to carefully manipulate the fragile and edematous bowel loops, aiming to preserve intestinal integrity and prevent iatrogenic injury during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon identifies the trite's ligament and continues laparoscopic exploration towards the ileocecal valve, searching for pathological changes along the bowel.

The surgeon ceases exploration due to the incarceration of a small intestine segment and switches to another view to explore the distal parts. Upon detecting the pathology, the intestines are re-evaluated from the terminal ileum up to the lesion, with no additional pathology observed.

Congenital bands are observed approximately ten centimeters proximal to the ileocecal valve, visibly strangulating the related segment of the intestine. These bands originate from the right lateral abdominal wall and extend towards the cecum.
The surgeon divides the congenital bands using ligature, reducing the internal herniation and freeing the passage of the bowel loops.
The surgeon assesses the jejunal segment, confirming sufficient blood circulation and noting the absence of additional intra-abdominal pathology.
The surgeon removes the trocars under direct vision, completing the surgery without any visible complications.
Postoperative course was uneventful.
Bowel movement started on postoperative day one and the patient was discharged on postoperative day two.
